data.title: Enel For 2026: I Consider This To Be 'Done'
data.description:
I consider the utility business Enel (ENLAY)(ESOCF) to be one of my best utility investments made in many years. Enel was a high-conviction investment that I bought when the company was at a considerably cheap share price. My initial position was bought at a share price of €4.3/share, which means that on the basis of the current share price above €9/share, I have made a 100% RoR on a utility investment. These investments are more typically reserved for yield and stability, not capital appreciation - but it showcases what can happen if you're cognizant of valuation, and you act on it.

My YoC was over 9%. The yield now is close to 5%. The company has solid fundamentals with a BBB-rating in credit, and a current market cap that's approaching almost €100B as it stands. It's wrong to call the company a business that, over the course of its history, has deserved a premium valuation. Rather, I believe it tends towards a conservative overall valuation - no more than about 12-13x P/E at best. That makes the above-13.3x P/E here a clear, at the very least, full valuation, and based on forecasts, I also consider it to be overvalued here.

This is a very solid sort of company. Despite some forecast uncertainty, its operating specifics are incredibly solid. It's very unlikely, given current utility and electrification trends in its home markets, in Europe, and in its other operating areas, to see a significant downturn.

But the current valuation has brought Enel to a price where I don't consider it to be in any way defensible to be invested in or bought. Despite a 10% RoR since my last article, I don't consider the company to be likely to outperform further - and even if it does outperform further, I would view that as an overvaluation.

Given the largely negative earnings and valuation trends we have today in terms of an overall inflated market, I would view the company as problematic to invest in.

Let me show you why.

Enel - The company's results were good, but forward growth (significant) seems very unlikely here

The company is set to report its CMD in about a month, and 4Q results in not too long, but I argue that even if the company's 4Q25 results are good, this would not provide a meaningful further upside to the business. We can see the recent 3Q25 results as confirmation of this. EBITDA was only up about 1%, with net income up about 5%. The total return for shareholders is very impressive, even according to the company's own calculations, which put it at 76% from '23 to '25, and this is an example of what I enjoyed during the last few years.

Unfortunately, this in no way means that the company is likely to continue this sort of performance here.

Enel is a play primarily on Italy, but equally much in LATAM as well as Spain (which together make up about a corresponding amount of EBITDA as Italy itself). The company has exposure to some of the more volatile operating geographies in LATAM, including Colombia, but it also has US exposure. For 2025, it's expected to hit its EBITDA as well as the net income target, which would put it at a native AEPS of about €0.7/share, with the yield at €0.47/share at the current period. This is relatively high, but something the company can also easily handle given its current finances. It has low leverage - about 2.5x net debt/EBITDA—and it has done most of the CapEx necessary to ensure an efficient and attractive cost base.

That the company is likely to see slight EBITDA improvements goes without saying, as is the fact that it's seeing better cash conversion and leverage improvements.

The US segments and the current FX situation mean that pairing and FX are currently a meaningful headwind for the company, but despite this, the latest results were very solid.

So why have I sold almost every single share in a company that is so solid, and has such stability?

Despite the company's good results, and despite the debt not being worrisome, its debt levels are meaningfully above both European and US peers of the same size. Rising interest rates in the Eurozone, which we are seeing and are likely to continue, are likely to contribute to higher borrowing and interest costs. The current pricing level for energy and electricity is also characterized by very high levels, which is providing Enel and other utilities with some very good tailwinds. In the event of lower demand, we can expect those tailwinds, and consequently the company's earnings and its dividend coverage, to drop, regardless of how good EBITDA growth (within reason) the company manages. Furthermore, European regulators are a bit on "edge" - I don't think it's unfair to characterize them as such. The combination of significant grid capacity bottlenecks due to legacy infrastructure (Italy is a good example of this, and 50%+ of the company is in Italy), supply chain pressure, volatility, the rise of what are known as distributed energy resources, or DERs, requires new ways of modulation and thinking for these companies - Enel is, as of today, not equipped to handle this (neither are others, so it's not an Enel-exclusive risk in any way).

But this is generally what prevents me from assigning these companies much higher valuations.

Despite significant investments on the part of Enel, the company is still required to invest significantly in infrastructure. Its plan may be insufficient, and the obvious consequence of this is that profits become lower. Furthermore, even if only slightly, the company is under the effect of share buybacks, which is adding to the share price inflation here.

Perhaps most important about my 2026-2028 result expectations, however, is the growth even in a good context - because the expectations for growth in this context, is not even that good. In fact, if you were to use the 20-year averages to estimate the forward P/E, then even with an annual AEPS growth of about 2%, the return you're getting here would be less than the yield of 5%, meaning the actual capital appreciation would be negative here.

Let me show you what I mean and what risks I see.

Valuation for Enel and the risks here

So, first off, the downside risk at this valuation for the 20-year average on a forward basis.

Please note that the overall likelihood for this forecast to materialize, based on historical accuracy, is relatively high. The company hits its targets about 70%+ of the time, and it's not forecasting much higher results than what you see here. So these returns, those are fairly certain, if the company trades at 12x. You could, of course, argue that Enel should trade significantly higher than this. But why?

The company doesn't provide any superior growth; it's not particularly higher rated - BBB, not higher - and it doesn't operate in any particularly safe geography or geography where it forecasts significant outperformance or explosive growth potential. No, rather its operating geographies are higher risk than others, with Spain and Italy as well as LATAM on the list, for most of its operating EBITDA and revenues.

The arguments for valuing this company significantly higher are therefore, to put it mildly, somewhat limited. The company's very capital-intensive operational nature, and utility-like growth (noted above) further confirm how low it, in my view, should be traded. The overall likelihood that regulators might allow higher returns in Europe is low in my view. This has to do with the general cost levels we're seeing also, but after the 2022 crisis in the energy sector, most national regulators are very careful in what they allow - in fact, price caps are not unheard of, which forces these utilities to find their earnings or profits in other ways (in Sweden, fees are added instead of actual pricing, for instance - it is not that expensive, but the associated fees are now higher than the power prices).

Also, given that this is very close to consumers' and voters' hearts, the political openness to even discuss more costs for utilities is almost nonexistent. Instead, countries are discussing further regulatory tools to constrain costs while allowing for investment.

It's a segment I love to invest in, but I will only do so at an attractive price - this is not an attractive price.

My previously stated PT for the company was €7/share. This comes to just below 10x P/E, and I stay with this PT. At below 10x P/E, and at below €7.1/share, the relative upside for this company on a forward basis, even on flat returns, is over 13-15% per year, depending on the company's AEPS growth. In this company, I am willing to take the risk of slightly sub-15% annualized returns because of the sheer safety that I get when investing here.

But beyond that, my interest in Enel is now at a multi-year low, and for 2026, I consider this company to be a very firm "HOLD", contrary to both analysts from SA and Quant considering it a "BUY" and "STRONG BUY" respectively.

I will say with fairness that I have a proven record of "going out" too early, however, so keep that in mind. It's something I am working on, but I don't see that risk here.

Risks & Considerations

With allowed network returns under the eye of regulators, I am specifically worried about the 2026-2031 period in Spain as well as the current state of the infrastructure in Italy. I went into detail with sources with regard to the Spanish regulator in my last article, but the Italian infrastructure investment needs are significant as well. This involves Enel as well as other major players like Terna.

Apart from that, EPS growth estimates of 2-3% might even be too positive, and this brings into question whether the company could really grow its dividend as expected - and I question whether the current growth is worth what's being expected for the business.

On the basis of this, I give the following thesis.

Thesis

My plans for holding Enel are simple. Buy it cheap - and hold it until/if it gets overvalued, which it can, and now it has been for a while. I have therefore been rotating, and have now finished rotating the company, barring a watchlist position.

Updated for 2026-2027E, the company is expected to generate stable returns and EPS growth (meaning less than 2-3% per year on average), but close to flat development as the CapEx peters out a bit, and with the changed strategy, I still guide for flat and anemic development. I state again, as I did in my previous article, Enel has reached its natural stopping point as an investment for me here, and I've been happy to sell. My rating of "HOLD" is for 2026-2028E reiterated, and 99% of the position has been sold. Barring my watchlist position, which I usually keep, I'm completely out for the time being.

I would consider a fair value of about €10/share to be possible here, but I wouldn't want to bet on the company actually managing to achieve this given the fundamentals and the company's operating specifics.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically is not enough. Based on this, and a price target of €7/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that is attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of January 2026.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: CIO Notebook: Fed Stands Firm As Powell's Successor Waits In The Wings
data.description:
By Shannon L. Saccocia, CFA

We remain constructive on risk assets, favoring small- and mid-cap equities, upgrading emerging market equity, adding to non-U.S. developed and emerging market debt, and maintaining overweights in commodities and private equity while staying neutral on private credit.

As expected, the Federal Open Market Committee (“FOMC”) held the fed funds target rate steady at 3.50% to 3.75% following their January meeting. After three dissents in the December meeting, only Christopher Waller and Stephen Miran dissented this meeting, with both calling for a 25-basis point cut. This marks the fourth straight dissent for the recently appointed governor whose term expires this Saturday, although there is no current word on the potential or timing of a replacement. As for Waller, while he is one of a small group of finalists to replace Fed Chair Jerome Powell, he has been on record with his continued concerns about the labor market. Worth noting, the Fed also added four new voters to the mix – Cleveland Fed President Beth Hammack, Philadelphia Fed President Anna Paulson, Dallas Fed President Lorie Logan, and Minneapolis Fed President Neel Kashkari. Cycling off were Susan Collins, Alberto Musalem, Austan Goolsbee, and Jeffrey Schmid.

There were some important, albeit not unpredicted, changes to the Fed’s statement. The FOMC noted that economic activity has accelerated at a “solid pace,” and while job gains have remained low, there have been “signs of stabilization” in the labor market. In addition, the statement pointed to a still elevated level of inflation but did not elaborate further on the trend. On closer inspection, it would appear that the FOMC has become more comfortable with the belief that the labor market is not on the precipice of further deterioration. While job gains have been admittedly underwhelming, there has been little growth in the supply of labor as well, which is helping to maintain a better balance than one would expect given the trailing three-month average for non-farm payrolls stands at -22k. Overall, the statement pointed to a more balanced view of the risks to the Fed’s dual mandate.

During the press conference, Powell acknowledged several concerns: housing has been “weak”, hiring is “low”, and the demand for workers has “clearly softened.” However, Powell reinforced the slightly more hawkish statement, stating that the Fed sits today within the estimates of the neutral range, albeit at the higher end in the eyes of some voters. For those looking for some dovish signs, Powell did state that the Fed continues to see tariff inflation as “one-time” and stated that “a lot of” the pass-through of higher tariffs has already occurred. This points to the ability for the Fed to act more decisively on any weakening in the labor market, akin to the actions the Fed took in 2025.

In addition to queries related to monetary policy, Powell fielded several questions about his recent attendance of the oral arguments in Governor Lisa Cook’s case before the Supreme Court, political pressure on the Fed and the DOJ case, his plans after his term as Chair concludes in May, and his views on the dollar – all of which resulted in no new information. He did state that he believes the Fed has maintained its credibility and that he and other Fed officials are committed to Fed independence, advising his successor to “stay out of elected politics” and remain engaged with Congress.

Today’s meeting did little to move markets or change minds on the path of rate cuts. With another one to two rate cuts likely this year and a global economy that is reaccelerating, our view on risk assets remains constructive. We are leaning into small and mid-caps and have upgraded emerging markets – especially India and Brazil – on better growth prospects and cheaper valuations. We are encouraging reallocating cash and recommend adding exposure to non-U.S. developed bonds and emerging market debt, where valuations and policy backdrops look more attractive. Within alternatives, we remain overweight commodities (notably gold and other precious metals) and private equity while staying neutral on private debt given rising credit risks.
